comments.title: Prayer and exercises
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
You warn against doing magic for people without their consent and I can see why. Prayer is a magical communication and is a spell of sorts. I have been getting great results praying for family and friends and they all like it. What are the ethical implications of praying for someone who does not know and has not given permission?

I am working out of your Druidry Handbook and Druid Magic Handbook. I know beginners tend to over do it and would like to know how many exercises such as a solar current or a telluric current, Druid's Egg or colour breathing would be too much?

Thank,
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Prayer and exercises
replies.content:
1) I wouldn't do it. I've been prayed at by Christians trying to convert me -- in one case, by a whole church of them -- and it was indistinguishable from any other kind of magical attack meant to control someone's will. (The energies from the church in particular felt dank and slimy; I don't know what they were praying to when they thought they were invoking Jesus, but it wasn't a power I'd want anything to do with.) Always ask permission before invoking spiritual forces for anyone else.

2) Take up one at a time, practice it until you can do it without having to think about the details, and then add another. You can do everything in both books at the same time, but don't try to learn them all at once!

replies.title: Re: Prayer and exercises
replies.content:
I have been requesting protection for my three kids specifically, and generally for my family, since I began the work. In the course of the ritual, I say, with arms crossed over my heart in the sign language sign for love, "protect my daughter x, protect my son y, protect my son z, protect all my loved ones", and may think specifically of one or another of them if they are particularly troubled by anything.

The intention was never to control the course of their lifetime in any way, but rather to ask that they not be a target of malefic forces that may attempt to get to me through them. In other words, I want specifically to exclude them from anything negative I may attract by doing the work.

My daughter knows of this and has no problem with it. My two sons are not interested in anything religious, so I did not ask. However, they both know I love them and want them to be well and happy, and would probably roll their eyes and say that mom is being mom. I have no intention of interfering with their lives beyond this. They are all three adults.

Is that OK?

Thank-you.
Myriam

replies.title: Re: Prayer and exercises
replies.content: I'd encourage you to ask your sons' permission. You can certainly say something on the order of "I know you don't believe in this stuff, but I do, and would you terribly mind if I pray for your well-being?" Or something like that. Consent really is important in this sort of thing.

replies.title: Re: Prayer and exercises
replies.content:
For what it might be worth... As a Christian (Catholic) I happen to agree that praying for someone, and especially for something specific for that particular someone, can easily be an unwanted intervention. But I have found a formula (from a channel I won't name) which can readily be used by anyone in order to express compassion in the most general way.

You can simply ask God (or angels or beings of your chosen way… or in the original formula, one calls upon the “most benevolent beings present or nearest”) to help or assist so-and-so in their time of need — “in the most benevolent ways possible, for the sake of the most benevolent outcome” — and you leave it at that. As a Christian, I follow that basic supplication with the triple prayer (Our Father, Hail Mary, Glory Be) in order to seal it, and make it acceptable to the benevolent beings attending.

Good for all occasions, and one leaves open every possibility for the true needs or issues to be met, since what is truly “benevolent” in each case is beyond our knowing, and yet the prayer offers a way of loving compassionate intercession, so that one feels one has “done” something for someone else. (And I apply it to everything, including natural disasters.)

What do you think, Mr. Greer?

best regards,
Petrus

replies.title: Re: Prayer and exercises
replies.content:
If I may, I'd like to insert my favorite Alan Watts quote here: “Kindly let me help you or you will drown,” said the monkey putting the fish safely up a tree.

As for a church of Christians praying to convert the Archdruid. Oh my! Dank and slimy energies...maybe they are secretly a church of Cthulhu worshipers? ;-)

replies.title: Re: Prayer and exercises
replies.content: Nah, if they were worshiping Great Cthulhu we'd get along just fine. ;-)  Did you ever notice that Lovecraftian cultists don't proselytize? They have the same attitude that the old Pagans had, and that Druids have today: if you'd like to share in our teachings and our work you're welcome, but if you're not, that's okay too.

comments.title: Lord's Prayer
comments.content:
Sorry, I just remembered this one. I attend an Anglican church and they say the Lord's Prayer, the one that goes "lead us not into temptation but deliver s from evil."
Is that prayer a Christian invoking and banishing ritual? It sounds like it to me. Do you think it would be effective as a banishing ritual?
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Lord's Prayer
replies.content: It's not a banishing ritual but it is a good solid prayer with protective effects. Most schools of Christian occultism advise saying it at least once a day, and that strikes me as a very good idea.

replies.title: Re: Lord's Prayer
replies.content: I have a Jewish friend who at one point attended a public school that required students to say the Lord's Prayer in the morning. (this would have been in the 1950s) She told me that she thought it over and decided that there was nothing in the prayer that a Jew couldn't say--after all it is addressed to God as Father, not to Jesus. So, without even consulting her parents on this dilemma, she went along with the program. Then years later she mentioned this to her father. He told her that he had the same situation as a Jewish boy in Oklahoma and came to the same conclusion.

comments.title: Filling in the blanks
comments.content:
JMG, in November of 2016 you wrote:

“Then we go to the other extreme, to those systems of divination that require no special states of consciousness, because they rely on cyclical phenomena that, at least in theory, have objective effects on human consciousness and thus on human affairs. There are a number of divination systems in this category, but the one that I plan to discuss—partly because it’s the one with which I’ve had a decent amount of experience, partly because it has had an immense role in the occult traditions of the western world—is astrology: “the speech of the stars,” to give the combination astro-logos its actual meaning in ancient Greek.”

To satisfy my seemingly endless curiosity, could you possibly enumerate and elaborate on some other systems of cyclical phenomena that fit into the same classification as the various schools of astrology?

replies.title: Re: Filling in the blanks
replies.content: I'm not familiar with a great many of them, as astrology has filled that role in the Western world pretty much to the brim. One I do know of is Nine Star Ki, which is an east Asian system still extensively practiced in Japan, and tracks a series of nested calendar cycles in which days, months, years, and so on each take on one of nine characteristics which interface with your birth characteristic. There are also offshoots of the I Ching that map the cycle of hexagrams onto the cycle of the year. There are doubtless others, but it's not something I've studied extensively.

comments.title: Magic, Elements and Sex
comments.content:
Well here's a weird one.

I occasionally experience sexsomnia, that is, having sex while asleep. Or, really, initiating sex-- Typically I wake up as the events get underway, by which time both I and my partner are committed to the course.

I discovered during the course of the Ovate grade of the DOGD that my sexsomniac episodes are affected by my magical practice. During the roughly 10 months I spent in the Ovate grade, I recorded 6 sexsomniac episodes in my journal. 4 occurred on nights that I had pore-breathed elemental fire during my meditation, 1 occurred after breathing Air, 1 after breathing Spirit, and no episodes at all occurred after working with either Earth or Water.

Further documentation over the course of the Bardic grade bears out the initial findings. Visits to the Fire grove bring on sexsomnia, as does pore breathing Fire, Air or Spirit. Moreover, regular work with a particular element also affects HOW my sexuality expresses itself. When I work with Fire regularly, I become more aggressive; when working with Water, more receptive. Not, I should note, at the level of deliberate consciousness-- I mean what I respond to sexually changes, depending on my engagement with the elements.

Have you ever heard of anything like this?

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: No, but one thing I have learned over the years is that magical energies express themselves in individual lives in very, very idiosyncratic ways. It's fair to say that no two people have exactly the same response to magical practices, and while your response isn't something I've heard of before, it's far from the weirdest thing I've heard of.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: What is the weirdest then?

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: Unfortunately telling the story, even with the names removed, would break a confidence.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: What's the weirdest you can talk about then?

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: Okay, how 'bout the second or third weirdest.  I'm in the mood for a good story.

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: Um, this is Magic Monday, not the Children's Occult Story Hour. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Magic, Elements and Sex
replies.content: Thanks JMG. Been wondering about this one for a long time...

comments.title: Flashes of light
comments.content:
JMG,

First, thanks for your answer last week. I followed your advice and ended up making several changes on my mentor’s recommendation.

One of the recommendations was to not wear my smart watch to bed, so I quit. A couple of nights after I quit, I turned out the light, but before going to sleep, I reached over with my left hand to move a book into a less precarious position on the bedside table.

As I put out my hand, flashes of white-yellow light edged in green came from my palm and fingers, and lit the darkness a kind of blue. It happened several times, but much more quickly than my description makes it sound.

Definitely a first for me. I asked my mentor while meditating, and apparently I had a glimpse of my light body (which I haven’t run across in my studies). I don’t know the proper terms, because the communication is instant and visual, and I often translate to words later.

replies.title: Re: Flashes of light
replies.content: That does indeed sound like you've gotten a glimpse of one of the layers of your light body -- this is also known as the aura. It's nothing to worry about, or focus on too intently; such glimpses sometimes let you know that you're going to develop clairvoyant gifts over time, but those are perfectly natural and will develop in their own time.

replies.title: Re: Flashes of light
replies.content:
What is the light body and how can we experience it?

Many thanks

replies.title: Re: Flashes of light
replies.content: The body of subtle energies that surrounds the physical body. There are various ways to experience it, but I don't recommend spending your time doing that -- you're better off focusing on general spiritual development and letting your subtle senses develop at their own pace.

comments.title: No question, just a comment
comments.content:
If you go to the wiki article for the Temple of Julius Caesar, you’ll find a photo of the altar in the ruins of that temple: basically some masonry with a heap of dirt on it (the marble and travertine were stripped away centuries ago). But over this altar are many flowers freshly placed. Apparently, someone in Rome is *still* making offerings to Divus Iulius in the temple built to him by Augustus. Some traditions seem to have lasting power.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: No question, just a comment
replies.content: Delighted to hear it.

comments.title: Franz Bardon
comments.content:
Last week you mentioned you wouldn’t recommend Franz Bardon’s books to beginners.

What are the dangers or issues with his methods which make it not recommended to beginners in your opinion?

What level of training and/or skill would you recommend one should attain to before pursuing initiation according to Bardon’s method as described in Initiation into Hermetics?

Thank you for the opportunity to ask questions.

replies.title: Re: Franz Bardon
replies.content:
The difficulty with Bardon's work for beginners is twofold. First, most novices who start out with Bardon, without any previous magical practice, bog down permanently in the first two steps of his system; it's a lot to take on when you have no previous background in magical work. Having a year or so of practice in less demanding systems makes the transition to Bardon's process of self-initiation a good deal easier.

Second, Bardon provides the beginner with very few options for dealing with the unpleasant magical energies that are so common in today's world. (If you're an advanced practitioner of Bardon's work, that's quite another matter, but we're talking about beginners here.) I've mentioned before that I consider daily practice of a good banishing ritual essential for the mage in training, and of course the central European traditions from which Bardon's work derives don't include those. Here again, a year or so of practice with a system that includes a functional banishing ritual would be extremely helpful.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Concerning my question from last Monday, I would like to ask what kind of patterns and circumstances can cause one to be reincarnated in a body with corporeal or mental disabilities? Thanks! Booklover

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's like asking "what causes physical and mental disabilities?" A vast range of things can be involved.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
1) Now this is a serious question, Archdruid. What are the magical implications of watching pornography? I have read some conflicting opinions about it, from it not being a big deal, to it causing serious harm to one's etheric body. Could you lay this issue to rest for me?

2) If I wish to see etheric energies (as a form of clairvoyance I suppose) what is the best exercise/training regiment to achieve this ability?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I don't have a lot of personal experience of that -- well, not since my teen years, at least, and the stuff I could get then was pretty mild by today's standards! -- but what I've seen leads me to think that it's no big deal.

2) If you're already engaged in a specific system of practices, stick with those until you've completed them. Chasing after specific abilities (such as etheric vision) is a common way for students to run off the rails.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you for the answers, John.

I will be sticking with the Druid Magic Handbook practices. I guess I am just being impatient.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: 1) but you previously said one shouldn't masturbate thinking about other people. Did you mean just "people known in person", and, in any case, what's the problem?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, the earlier question was about people you know, and the problem is that your concentration on them could communicate what you're doing, in one form or another, to their minds if they have any psychic capacities.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey John
Are you familiar with the divination system called napoleons book of fate? It appears to be some kind of simplified geomancy which only gives prewritten answers to a handful of prerecord d general questions. (E.g “ shall I gain or lose in my cause?”)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yep. There used to be quite a few systems like that in early modern pop culture. I never got much out of them, but you can certainly experiment with them if you like.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey jmg
When you do the beginner grade in the golden dawn how exactly are you supposed to meditate on the zodiac? I’ve been simply visualising the zodiac signs will remember their characteristics, is that the correct way?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, it's a lot more complex than that. I'd encourage you to read my book Paths of Wisdom, which has a chapter on the subject of magical meditation. (It also covers all the Cabalistic lore you'll need in your Golden Dawn studies, which might be useful as well.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I have that book, are you saying I’m supposed to meditate on the zodiac in the same way you meditate on sephiroth?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Exactly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Do your books contain discussions of various *different* types of meditation? If so, which ones?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. I talk about the form of meditation I practice and teach, discursive meditation, and just that form.

comments.title: An Old Man's Prophecy
comments.content:
Hello again JMG,

Sometime ago you advised me to go to the Theosophical society because of an issue I was having. Its taken some time for me to work up the nerve to ask for help there, but that is ongoing. However, while going there an old man told me in hushed tones that I had an exceptional spiritual gift. I humored him and asked him "how so?" and he said that I had the potential to be a medium. I didn't know what to make of that, and left feeling troubled. Having known a number of people who were told strange things by old men, I took his assertion with a grain of salt. Part of me wondered, though, if there was any truth to what he said, at all. The next day I got out a paper and a pen, and thought of a figure I had seen while meditating, and attempted something I have since learned is called automatic writing. My written queries got responses in a cursive hand and I got really weirded out, and worried that I was getting myself into more trouble. The information I found about automatic writing was primarily either Christians saying it was diabolical, or else people claiming to be psychic saying it was perfectly safe. So my question is, is this something that is safe to pursue, and do I need to protect myself in some way?

Thank you for taking the time to answer all of these questions.

replies.title: Re: An Old Man's Prophecy
replies.content: Automatic writing is one of the safest of mediumistic practices, but you might want to talk to your Theosophical contacts about the subject, since any help they are giving you may be impacted by any additional spiritual practices.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Good day Mr Greer,


Agrippa’s “On Geomancy” says ”...Caput Draconis is the sixth House increaseth sicknesses and diseases...”
Indeed it does look like Caput Draconis could well mean the beginning or possibility of disease.

In “The Art and Practice of Geomancy “ (page 159) you say "...Caput Draconis indicates the querent’s health is about to change for the better..."
How is that so?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Tell you what -- go pick up half a dozen books on the Tarot and see if all of them say exactly the same thing about any given card. Books disagree with one another; that's why it's important to study the cards, or the geomantic figures, and develop your own personal understanding of the divinatory symbols you're using.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
OK,
Sixth house has to do with disease.
Health is our default option.
Disease ‘settles in, disease ‘begins’.
If something is a ‘possibility’ that would be the disease.

Opinions on a same subject vary. They sure aren’t ‘exactly the same’.
But your take was exactly “the opposite”.

So for the sake of clarification could you elaborate on the reasons that led you to attribute this meaning to Caput Draconis in sixth?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Because that's what it consistently turned out to mean in the divinations I cast, of course.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
"...it's important to study the cards, or the geomantic figures, and develop your own personal understanding..."

I suppose I agree with that in spirit, since our affinities are individual more than generic... although it leaves me intrigued as to why Jung advised would-be diviners to read through the entire text for all the hexagrams before beginning to use the I Ching...

Petrus

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: With an unfamiliar oracle, that's a good idea. If you were to take up Tarot cards for the first time, I'd encourage you to read through the little white book that comes with the cards, and then put it away and pay attention to what the images mean to you. Reading through the whole text helps prime your subconscious mind for the work of divination.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Thanks, although I had a feeling you'd say something like that. Thing is, I've used a number of different versions of the I Ching over the years, without ever reading any of them through first, and I usually got answers which made sense or provided the necessary clarity...

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: And that demonstrates my point as well: Carl Jung's advice, good as it doubtless was for many other people, wasn't necessary for you.

comments.title: Protective Wash for Carpet?
comments.content:
Hello, and thank you for the generous sharing of your learning.

Do you have any suggestions for executing a protective floor wash in an entirely carpeted house? The place we're renting has carpet everywhere, even in the kitchen and bathroom (yea, it's awful. Even more so with two big fluffy dogs, but it's all we could find to rent that we could afford, close enough to work and would take our dogs. The housing situation in Maine is seriously messed up for anyone who's not elite.) It's become apparent to me while living here that the place os full of some nasty, nasty mojo - the former inhabitants being a long string of thieves, addicts, and general lowlives. I'm hoping it (and we) will benefit from some sort of cleansing, but mopping isn't really in the cards here. It's bad enough sometimes that we've considered sleeping in a tent on warm nights.

Many thanks for any guidance.
CK

replies.title: Re: Protective Wash for Carpet?
replies.content: Not to step on JMG's response by any means, but what occurs to me is a smoke purification, known in the Heathen trads as recaning and in the Celtic as saining. Juniper is traditional for both, and you're meant to fill the space with smoke as much as you can stand (don't forget to turn off the smoke alarms!). It might also help to make a banishing powder, sprinkle it over the carpets, and vacuum it up. After either purification, asperge the place generously with blessed water, paying particular attention to the corners, and hang up some kind of protective talisman, like a Brigid's Cross, a Troll Cross, or the like and invoke the blessings of your ancestors or whichever of the Holy Powers you know.

replies.title: Re: Protective Wash for Carpet?
replies.content: Also not intending to step on JMG's response, but the first thing I thought of would be baking soda mixed with cleansing/banishing dried herbs powdered up and mixed in, like a magical version of CarpetFresh, that would be vacuumed up after a little while.  It might also work for getting funky odors out!  I know that whenever I moved into a new room in the (Army) barracks, a decent cleaning was in order, and once it smelled better it usually "felt" better, so in my experience bad vibes are linked to an underlying funky smell.

replies.title: Re: Protective Wash for Carpet?
replies.content: Get one of those spray bottles that you use to do mist watering for plant leaves, and put the floor wash in that; spray a light mist of your floor wash from a foot or so above the carpet, and make sure the whole carpet gets a light misting. You can also splash Florida water into the four corners of each room, and burn a purifying incense such as frankincense.

replies.title: Re: Protective Wash for Carpet?
replies.content: Ok, that seems entirely self-evident now, and I feel a bit silly for not seeing it myself... I will do these things, and I hope they will help. Thank you!

comments.title: Natural magic and natal astrology
comments.content: I've recently acquired your handbook on natural magic and am using it to pick out some materials to work with. I've picked out some planetary and astrological influences that also appear in my natal horoscope, in the hope that this will make the results more 'personalised'. Is this a solid theory or is it more likely to reinforce my pre-existing bad habits? Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and natal astrology
replies.content: Well, what is your intention? Natural magic isn't decorative; for each working you do, you should have a specific intention, and you should pick the materials you use on the basis of the intention you have, not on the basis of personalization.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and natal astrology
replies.content:
My intention is to develop serenity and confidence in myself; I have some long-standing anxiety that I want to fix. I've picked out Virgo as an astrological influence and Venus as a planetary one; Virgo is also my sun sign. Saturn and Neptune feature in my natal horoscope so I've picked those Virgo materials that also have aspects of those planets to them.

Thank you for your help.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and natal astrology
replies.content: That shouldn't be any kind of a problem.

comments.title: Elemental Cross / SoP With Egyptian Gods/Goddess
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I am at the beginning/practical phase of the "Druid Magic Handbook". In the practical exercises in the Druid Handbook (i.e Elemental Cross, SoP, etc) I would like to know your thoughts on working with a set of Egyptian Gods / Goddesses in place of Celtic's. I find myself more drawn towards Egyptian Pantheons.


Also in the Druid Magic Handbook and The Druidry Handbook there is no mention of working with the different phases of the moon. For example as it regards to invoking the different powers represented by the full moon. Please shed some light on this.

Finally, I have your Sacred Geometry Oracle. How could one use the sacred geometry oracle as a complement to the the Ogham divination?

Thank you very much,

Peace and Blessings from London, UK

replies.title: Re: Elemental Cross / SoP With Egyptian Gods/Goddess
replies.content:
Tell me this. If you decided to bake a cake and had never made the recipe before, and you decided on a whim to replace 500 grams of sugar with 500 grams of salt, do you think the resulting cake would be edible?

A magical system isn't just a grab bag of things pieced together on the basis of personal preference. Each element is there for a reason. MY recommendation is that you do the training program exactly as given, all the way through, and then you'll know enough about magic to know what you can replace and how.

comments.title: Wild Wild Country
comments.content:
Hello JMG!

I'm watching the documentary on Osho's former Oregon community, and certainly you were aware of it at the time. The whole thing striked me both as an amazing and absolutely unbalanced work of magic. Such immense and sudden work of transformation was certain to amass imense resistance! What's your take on it from an occult/magic perspective?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Wild Wild Country
replies.content: That kind of thing is very common in the history of alternative religion. One of the routine thing that happens when religious communities head out into rural country to set up some kind of would-be utopia. Every action produces an unequal and opposite reaction -- so, yes, things very often spin out of control in colorfully dysfunctional ways.

comments.title: The land
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

I had the time today to listen to your latest podcast and thoroughly enjoyed it. Thanks for taking the time to take part in such endeavours. I expect that they're a magical act for you.

I've been curious about something here for a while and would be interested in your opinion, if you could please indulge me?

I'm finding that the land here on the farm, sort of (and I don't really know what the English words are to describe this form of insight) speaks to me. For example, I get insight as to what is possible here. And I have absolutely no idea where such inspiration comes from, but it does. Now of course, as you know, I am adding my own perspiration to that inspiration, so it is all to good effect, but it is a guided act of creation.

But what I wanted to know is, what sort of magic is this, and do you know of anyone who practices it and has written about it?

That's actually three questions... Sorry, I am nothing if not cheeky!

Cheers

Chris

replies.title: Re: The land
replies.content: Have you read any of the books on Findhorn, the very strange farm in Scotland? That's the most famous example of this sort of thing . Yes, it's very common; I don't know that it has a specific name, but it's very much a real thing, and I suspect most good farmers practice it to some extent or another.

replies.title: Re: The land
replies.content:
Interestingly, my question is related to this.

I garden actively and have lived the pagan wheel of the year for half my adult life. I have found that land, and trees, and animals speak to me sometimes too.

As an example: Once, as a teenager, trees told me to climb them! It shocked me, but I complied-- and then a pack of dogs who were being trained to silently hunt down PEOPLE (for some official policing thing) came out of nowhere and attacked. But I was already in the tree, and unharmed until the trainers came and got the dogs.

So... yeah! Absolutely! I still get surprised and puzzled by what I find.

comments.title: Invoking Deities
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'm working through the Candidate grade of the AODA using the Druidry Handbook. Two questions:

1) I'm not clear on how to go about invoking deities. A number of other folks have written in here about things not to do, which has been helpful, but could you give a couple of pointers in what to do? Also, is invoking deities something typically reserved for grove ceremonies, or done daily as part of the SOP ritual?

2) I won't bore you with the details, but I've had a couple of meditative experiences linking me to the Celtic deities, specifically from the Irish tradition. The trouble is, the Irish mythology sources seem particularly sketchy and muddled compared to, say, the ancient Greek pantheon, which have a vast amount of material to draw on. So I find myself trying to define a relationship with deities that are quite unclear. Do you have any advice for how to proceed?

Thanks as always for sharing your time an expertise here!

replies.title: Re: Invoking Deities
replies.content:
1) It's called "prayer." You have a conversation with the deity and listen for the answer. You can certainly do it in or out of ritual.

2) Your best bet is to pray to the deities directly, and ask them!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello and happy Monday,

There is a park in my neighbourhood that a famous person once ran through, and there are stones marking the path he took. It's a very well known route, and is taught in history classes here. Can such a thing give a piece of land an egregore? Do land spirits and egregores tend to get along?

Thank you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, and it depends on the land spirits.

comments.title: Antipodean SOP
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I am working through TDMH and based in Australia. Two questions.

1. I am sure you have said in the past that altering the directions of the gates is ok. I felt a strong pull to rotate 90 degrees so Air is north, Fire east, Water south and Earth west. Here on the east coast those directions make some logical sense too. Just want to confirm that's not a rookie error.

2. Invoking by stags,salmons and bears doesn't make a lot of sense to me. Over a period of months alternatives have occurred to me. Welcome Swallows for Air. Tortoise for Water. Red Kangaroo for Fire. Great serpent for Earth. Again, is this advisable?

Cheers

replies.title: Re: Antipodean SOP
replies.content: Yes, it's a rookie error. I'd encourage you to do the practices exactly as given until you have completed the course of training; then you'll know enough about magic to be able to make appropriate modifications to the system.

replies.title: Re: Antipodean SOP
replies.content:
Sorry to barge in here but is there a danger if you do the practices in the wrong direction? I'm in Australia too and here we get all sorts of conflicting advice about what direction to face and that you should do thing anti-clockwise instead of clockwise etc.

If the direction is wrong could you end up having the opposite effect e.g. you think you're banishing something but you're actually invoking it?

replies.title: Re: Antipodean SOP
replies.content: Well, the Golden Dawn temple in New Zealand did the rituals exactly the same way as in Britain, and got good results, so it's probably safe to keep on doing it the traditional way despite what the conflicting advice says!

comments.title: Active Suppression
comments.content:
Since I have been reading about witch-stuff a lot lately (The Cunning Man book is a great source) I am beginning to think that maybe a gauge of validity of an alternate way of viewing and interacting with the world around us is the amount of effort that the "normals" of the world put into repressing the alternate method.

Witchcraft was fairly savagely repressed in the day. Even now, if you sit down with a fundamentalist Christian and mention Witchcraft you will certainly get a earful about "evil" and a need to stamp it out.

Mostly the repression now is of a more urbane variety. Subtle mocking and a call out to science for "proof".

That being said, I have been looking into Wilhelm Reich and "Orgone" and found that idea somewhat interesting, but since the guv'mint took it on itself to burn all the data, I have minimal ability to assess the claims. And the claims themselves seem kinda odd. But truth is sometimes found in "Odd".

What is your opinion about the relationship of level of repression to possible utility?

Do you know anything about Orgone?

replies.title: Re: Active Suppression
replies.content: The more useful something is, the more likely it is to be repressed if it conflicts with the political, economic, and social hierarchies of the time. As for orgone, I haven't studied it in detail, but it seems to be the same thing as qi, ki, prana, vril, etc., etc. -- that is to say, the life force that every spiritual tradition talks about.

replies.title: Re: Active Suppression--orgone
replies.content: Robert Anton Wilson had quite a bit to say about Reich's work. He actually writes about blundering across the FDA agents actually in the process of destroying Reich's books--which blew his (Wilson's ) mind. He wrote a play on the topic. Much interesting weirdness in RAWs work.

comments.title: Meditation
comments.content:
(From Dudleydawson)

I got the feeling from your last article that you're not crazy about mindfulness meditation--or at least the version of mindfulness meditation that's commonly offered up in corporate environments as a stress-reliever. I researched a bit further and found a comment on the Whatever Happened to Transcendence article in which you mentioned that pursuing mindfulness meditation as a stress reliever (without the spiritual component) is roughly the same as drinking to the point of metal-numbness.

Your point seems legitimate to me, and I've given it a lot of thought over the last few days.

I'm an anxious person and I practice mindfulness meditation as a way to slow my thoughts down and relax. Without getting in to all the gory details, I'm trying to address my anxiety in several different ways, with the end-goal of being a more competent, composed, and measured person. I say that only to establish the point that I'm not doing this as a way of achieving a chemical-free high that enables me to suffer through the miseries that I don't care to fix or address.

...Which brings me to discursive meditation and magic in general.

In the Whatever Happened to Transcendence post, you mention the lack of people that want to "tear open the sky and step into the luminous beyond," (I apologize if I'm mangling that quote; I don't actually have it in front of me). That sentiment appeals to me.

I've lived life as a non-believer for a long time, not necessarily by choice. The more you talk about magic, though, the more I find myself nodding along, getting interested, an opening my mind to the idea that there's potential lurking within me and in the world at large that I'm missing. So, I may be on the verge of some new explorations.

In your opinion, what does Magic have to offer the human mind?

Can it help sharpen and focus a person, or are sharpness and focus things that you should bring to the table when you begin studying magic?

I've heard you put this in terms of "changing consciousness" in the past, I think. Can you explain that a bit?

Could this pursuit offer new ways for me to consider and address the anxieties I mentioned earlier? Or should my anxiety keep me from practicing in the first place (until I get them sorted)?

I apologize. I'm sure this is coming off as gushy and probably very amateur/noob-ish. Those would all be accurate terms. New possibilities are presenting themselves and I can't help but be a bit excited.

And if you’ll indulge me in one last question…Have you ever used discursive meditation to work through a writing project?

Okay, I’m done. Thanks for your patience, provided you’ve managed to maintain it!

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
Oh, lord - it's even more monstrous in print. Sorry? I'll try to be a bit more tl;dr next time and keep it to a one question minimum.

-Dudley

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
If mindfulness meditation works for you, then by all means use it. Magic is a toolkit; you can do whatever you want to do with it. You can certainly use it to help sharpen and focus your mind and personality, and you can also use it to control your anxieties. It depends on what you want to do.

No, I don't use discursive meditation for my writing, but many of the skills I've learned doing discursive meditation have helped me in my writing.

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
Thank you. I picked up a copy of Learning Ritual Magic. Is that a good place to start?

I've also been reading along in The Cosmic Doctrine.

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content: Yep -- that's a solid basic introduction to ceremonial magic in the Golden Dawn tradition. Once you've completed everything in it, you can go on to my books Paths of Wisdom and Circles of Power, which will give you a thorough training in the whole body of Golden Dawn magic.

comments.title: prayer 101?
comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

Have you written for beginners about the practice of prayer? It's something that stumps me - I'm unsure if it's just the composing of a mental letter with a "dear deity" greeting, how it differs from the general "hello, anybody out there" mental loops that arise, etc. I'm kind of hamstrung at the thought of asking for anything (how do I know what's good, in the end?), am not much of a speak-out-loud kind of person, but can't imagine prayer is supposed to just be me, thinking thoughts.

Background: grew up in a Methodist church, didn't get prayer then (didn't get God, either) and felt out of place, wandered through life wishing for divine connection but ... well, general mundanity prevails; began the AODA 1st grade course as outlined in the DH (with addition of SOP, etc. from DMH) this past Alban Heruin. I've started with the work as provided in the two books (ie. Elemental Cross with Hu Gadarn, Esus, Ceridwen & Niwalen) and haven't found one pantheon more relevant than any other, so I'll just keep to what you've written until someone (as in a god or goddess) suggests otherwise. I just don't even know how to figure that out.

What might you recommend for a polytheist prayer primer?

Also, I suppose it's recommended to learn more about the deities with whom one is attempting to work - in this case, the few books I've found on Celtic legends haven't turned up much on the four deities listed above. Might you have some titles/sources you recommend?

Many thanks!

replies.title: Re: prayer 101?
replies.content: I haven't written much on it; there's a short chapter on the subject in my first book, Paths of Wisdom. There isn't much in print on any of the deities you've mentioned; you might consider simply praying to them and asking for their guidance.

replies.title: Re: prayer 101?
replies.content: Thank you. With "pray to them" I hit the snag of my first question. Do you mean, set up a mental conversation? How might one guarantee a connection with the right entity or differentiate between prayer and general thoughts of "hello, anybody there?......, gee, here I am echoing around in my head again..."? I've done that. It seems less than efficacious as it tends to affirm that I'm kind of a dunce. That may be the case, but too, maybe I want to pray for help becoming an un-dunce.

replies.title: Re: prayer 101?
replies.content: Set aside those worries and stray thoughts. Go ahead and pray as though the god you're invoking is listening. Do it daily -- talk to the god and then listen, and pay attention to what you hear. Praying for help becoming an un-dunce is a really good place to start, too!

replies.title: Re: prayer 101?
replies.content: Thank you. That's encouraging and actually made me grin. Will do!

comments.title: The dangers of Magic
comments.content:
Hello!

Last week, fluiddruid asked the following question: ".....the author warns that a person who begins to practice magic may attract attention of some "nasty" entities. I have never experienced anything like that, however all I do is banishing rituals. So I wonder what that passage was about."

And you answered the following: "Good question. You may just have been lucky. ;-)"

I got a bit worried there! So are there any risks of attracting undesirable entities by the regular practice of magic? If so, how do they manifest and what should we be doing then?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: The dangers of Magic
replies.content: Yes, you can attract malicious or mischievous entities; their manifestations can range from repetitive nightmares through stuff being knocked off shelves to visual experiences; and that's one of the reasons why daily banishing rituals are such a good idea if you're practicing magic.

comments.title: On Vipassana meditation.
comments.content: A friend of mine went on a Vipassana retreat last week and he wanted to start a weekly mindfulness session with our roomies (we're all recovering addicts and apparently it's quite good for the rehab process). I don't think it would have adverse effects on my progress with daily practice but I wanted to check with you. What do you think?

replies.title: Re: On Vipassana meditation.
replies.content: Does the version of mindfulness meditation he did involve the whole range of Buddhist Vipassana practice, or was it the watered-down form with all the Buddhist elements taken out? The former's a good idea, the latter can be very problematic.

comments.title: Glamour experiment
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Some feedback on your anti-glamour experiment, if that is still welcome?

As I mentioned in the original thread I wanted to try it with my elderly parents, particularly my dad who for years now would rather sit stupefied in front of his flat screen than interact with his grandkids.

Last Sunday I cellotaped a big safety pin to their internet router, which also brings the digital TV I believe. I recited the Kipling for good measure :-).

During yesterday's visit he did not put the TV on at all whilst we were there, only just as we were leaving, and what's more he had earlier in the week bought some new CDs and was blasting out old favourites which I have not seen him do in a long time. He had also been watching a lot of new DVDs which to my mind is an improvement as at least there's an element of choice. But again, the screen was dark the whole time we were there, and much more interaction with the kids. Very refreshing!

He had been very ill with alcohol-related problems for the longest time, but has this year been massively on the upswing anyway, so that must be factored in too. But I could not but be struck by the different behaviour in any case. I really hope it continues.

Thanks a million for that one!

Morfran

replies.title: Re: Glamour experiment
replies.content: Many thanks for this. This is good to hear!

comments.title: Banishing
comments.content:
Hi John,

Is there, or how would I go about creating, some kind of generic banishing ritual?

Same question for Sphere of Protection. (The movement of energy in the spine is different from a technique I already do)

In my younger days I was working with some friends clearing brush from land that was going to be built upon. One night, late, we drove up there (we slept there) and there was a very strong feeling of antipathy and unwelcome; not really directed towards us or what we were doing but just malignant in general. I raised my hands palms out and told these energies to depart while turning in the four directions. When I was done we felt it to be cleared and that was the end of it. When I think about it now I think maybe I was just really lucky I didn't get fried.

Anyway, some sort of banishing/protection seems like a very good thing these days with the seeming increase of yucky energies being bandied about.

I'm not a Druid but I do believe in magic.

replies.title: Re: Banishing
replies.content: You can't have a generic banishing ritual, because a banishing ritual calls on some specific set of energies -- that's its power source. You need to choose a specific set of powers to invoke, and go with that.

comments.title: middle pillar
comments.content:
Archdruid,

I noticed you put up versions of the middle pillar exercise and banishing ritual of the lesser pentegram. I'm currently practicing from the druid magic handbook and druid's handbook, should I also be doing the middle pillar and banishing ritual? I do the SoP twice a day so not sure if that is the same as lesser pentegram.

Regards,

Syfen
.

replies.title: Re: middle pillar
replies.content: You've already learned a good banishing ritual -- that's one of the things the Sphere of Protection does. Don't worry about adding in the Pentagram ritual and the Middle Pillar; the SoP and the rest of the material in The Druid Magic Handbook is all you need.

replies.title: Re: middle pillar
replies.content: Great, thank you!

comments.title: Dark Night
comments.content:
JMG - I'm curious as to what you think about St John's (of the Cross) concept of The Dark Night of the Soul, i.e., the mundane world's "pushback" against those who seriously undertake the spiritual journey. The Catholic Church, at least at one time, emphasized the Dark Night, maybe sometimes to morbid excess; I think the Church's Marian Mvt. still does to an extent. I don't know the particulars, but I do know Buddhism has its own version of the Dark Night. From what I've observed, most modern Christian denominations don't mention it at all or tend to breeze over it. In any event, the Dark Night, it is said, can be rather severe, including just not psychological trials, but worldly ones such as health, financial, ethical and moral temptations and challenges, etc, anything to discourage the aspirant so that he or she quits the spiritual pursuit.

Is there a particular Druidic perspective re the Dark Night? You may have mentioned it in one of your books, I don't know. I recall reading in one of David Spangler's books that he believes the Dark Night trial is no longer necessary in the modern era, or at least not to the extent it once was, and thus he doesn't emphasize it all in his writings. I'm wondering if this is so, and if it is so, then to what can we attribute the change?

Thanks in advance!

Will M

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content: I've never met anyone who went through the Dark Night of the Soul who wasn't a Christian. It's apparently an issue only for people in that specific tradition.

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content:
Hmm, perhaps so. However, there are many who equate the Dark Night with the rising of the kundalini and the stress it can bring to bear on body and psyche. If this analogue is so - and I tend to think it is, though there's an obvious East/West cultural difference between the two - it would be difficult to believe the only people who experience the stressful K rising/Dark Night are Christians.

Of course Christians would term their experience a DNotS, those of another tradition would term it something else.

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content: I've read St. Juan de la Cruz, and I've also read a number of accounts of stressful kundalini awakenings, and attempts to equate the two seem unconvincing to me -- the distinctive features of the kundalini experience, to the best of my recollection, simply aren't there in Juan de la Cruz' account. For that matter, there are a lot of accounts of satori in Japanese Zen literature that have only the vaguest and most general points in common with either. I would argue that there are many different ways you can pursue mystical experience, and these ways have different results; it simply isn't true that all roads climb the same mountain! Rather, they climb different mountains and get different views from their respective summits.

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content:
I don't think the Dark Night is only Catholic. Tanya Luhrmann's book _When God Talks Back_ which is about the American Evangelical movement contains some interviews with people who experienced a loss of contact with God after having successfully achieved communication. These were people who were accustomed to an intense prayer practice, treating Jesus a a friend to talk things over with, even to the extent of pouring a cup of coffee for Him.

Interesting book. Luhrmann is an anthropologist who specializes in studying sub culture in which the member have to adopt a set of beliefs at odds with mainstream reality. Her first study was of ritual magicians and Witches in London, her second of Freudian psychiatrists in training.

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content: I'm quite sure it's not only Catholic; that's why I said that everyone I know who's been through it is a Christian -- that label includes Protestants, Orthodox, and several other broad divisions as well.

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content:
I was responding to the original questioner saying other denominations didn't seem to pay any attention to the idea. Interestingly, the evangelicals Luhrmann studied use Jesuit spiritual exercises.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Dark Night
replies.content: My apologies! I thought you were addressing my original response -- looking now I see I got the queueing order mixed up.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: In one of your older blog posts (I think it was in the Well of Galabes?), you mentioned a system of druidical numerology assigning specific meanings to the numbers 1 through 12. Is there somewhere this system is written out more completely?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The book that goes with Colin and Liz Murray's The Celtic Tree Oracle covers the basics. It's 1 through 13, by the way.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Isaac Hill here, so I have a few questions, answer what you will, thank you for having this open forum. First, is there any simple spell/practical magic I can do to affect winning a competition? There're good odds and I have 8 other people on my team with various experience in magic.

Second, more in depth, I'm still trying to figure out my path better- I've been working through Culadasa's The Mind Illuminated which is Samatha-vipassana Buddhist meditation, and it seems to be just what I need for progressing as directly as possible towards Awakening, but things have slowed down as other life stuff is happening. I am continuing the program obviously, and fully intend to complete the work, which will probably take several years, and I'm sure there will be many high and low points on the way. Its not a whole system though, in there's no rituals, spirit work, etc, just meditation. I don't identify as a Buddhist, and feel more called towards Western magick, so I'm doing a sort of hybrid by doing the LBRP(which I'll be changing to SOP soon) ancestor work, nature spirit work and so on. You have previously said that you don't recommend Buddhist meditation for westerners, and that emptying the mind isn't great, but in my experience I have seen it to be effective both for other people and myself, and I also see directly how most of my thoughts are rubbish (thinking about the past, future, my self image, etc) and to find Truth I need to go beyond the mind. That being said, in TMI Culadasa gives directions for Analytic meditation, so its something I can easily add to my daily practice and still jive with the program I'm on. I'm trying to dig deep into one thing and go the full way, so do you think analytic/discursive meditation on Buddhist concepts (ie 8 folk path, etc) is the way to go or does it matter? Like maybe meditating on the tree of life or astrology should wait a few years, but I could meditate on other stuff like my psychological patterns or something?

Ok, sorry if that was convoluted, trying to focus while having a whole heck of a lot going on (don't want to get into it here but I have a very full life)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first, I don't teach individual spells for anything but the occasional protection and healing working. I'm interested in teaching people how to make magic a part of their lives.

Second, that is to say, you want to practice a mismash of things taken from a random assortment of sources, without any clear idea how they might interact with one another. I don't recommend that, though I know that kind of thing is fashionable these days. Since that's not something I recommend at all, the only advice I can or will offer is to encourage you to consider finding one system of magical or spiritual training, and do that exactly as written, all the way through. Only after that will you have the background you would need to blend unrelated systems with any degree of safety. You can mess yourself over very thoroughly by mashing stuff together at random.

comments.title: secrecy
comments.content: Hi JMG, I think you've mentioned that secrecy is a powerful magical tool.  With that in mind, is it useful and/or common to keep an interest in magical philosophy and practice secret from one's spouse, or is it not such a good idea?

replies.title: Re: secrecy
replies.content: That depends entirely on your relationship with your spouse. Generally speaking, I don't think it's a good idea, but here as so often the personal element is the thing that matters.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
How long are souls usually out of incarnation? I have two sets of past life memories from two very clearly different lives, but the space between when one dies (1870 or so) and the next was born (1879) is fairly small.

I've taken all the precautions you recommended last week, which has lead to, among other things, me getting a fairly good background on the Meiji Restoration, something I'd never looked into in any detail but which formed the background of the earlier life. Both lives seem to check out on that basis. Memories from both lives also have me going "Oh, that's why..." a lot.

Is nine years long enough? I've come across Japanese teachings saying the soul is out of incarnation for at least thirty years between lives, so I'm curious if you think there's anything to it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It really does vary. Sometimes the gap is very short, sometimes it's a good deal longer. The gap between my last life and this one was apparently around three years; that's not healthy -- it's when the gap's too short that you get stuff bleeding over from life to life and causing various kinds of confusion -- but it does happen from time to time. Nine years wouldn't surprise me unduly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Do you know what determines the length of the gaps, or is it just something that happens?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Good question. We don't know.

comments.title: Loosely linked to the Physical, too Reflective
comments.content:
JMG,

Last week there was a discussion of children who are "loosely linked to the physical," and in early March there was a discussion of people who are more-than-usually reflective, including special considerations for child-rearing in the first case and practicing meditation in the second.

As a child (at least) I was definitely loosely linked with the physical, and have always been more-than-usually - and often overly - reflective. Additionally, I was a very sensitive child (to both physical and non-physical stimuli) and had to learn to "put up walls" and "shut down" that sensitivity in order to cope. My childhood circumstances led to a lot of wounding regarding others' perceptions of and reactions to my "specialness."

I'm now a healthy, normally functioning adult; though I still tend strongly toward the reflective and abstract, and when I first started practicing magic I had to take it slow while my sensitivity adjusted.

I have two-ish questions:

1) As an adult practicing magic and meditation, are there any special considerations I should be aware of regarding a loose link to the physical or being overly "tuned inward" and reflective?

2) I'm worried that the wall-building and shutting-down I did as a child might hamper my magical development as an adult. Are there safe ways to soften the barriers I made? I assume addressing the aforementioned wounds would help, and if so do you have any advice for that?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Loosely linked to the Physical, too Reflective
replies.content:
1) What was a problem for you as a child is an advantage as an adult studying magic.

2) The barriers will come down naturally as you develop inner strength through sustained magical practice; don't worry about them. Just pursue your practices, taking it as slow as you need to go in order to let yourself adjust, and you'll be fine.

comments.title: Acedia, among other things
comments.content:
Hi,

So as per my comment on your other post, going into the Army didn't work out for me, I didn't even get to ship out due to an injury to my foot, and I couldn't get a medical waiver. It was disappointing, but after finding out about the news, a couple of things happened. My mom broke down in tears because she was worried about me, much more so than my other two sisters who are already in the Army; she told me she had a dream that she was holding a dead baby and did not know why, and she took it as a sign from God that things would not have gone well with me in the Army. But I know nothing about dream interpretation.

I decided to do some divination using the Greek Alphabet Oracle system, with the tiles but in a bowl and tossed and five pieces land on the divination mat. The first tile signified a "difficult or unexpected result if you stray from your heart’s wisdom." The second suggested a strong desire to see results, and quickly. I interpret this to be my disappointment/sudden shock and wanting to do something about my situation immediately. The last three tiles fell close to each other and overlapped, and were fortuitous, suggesting I will ultimately succeed, and that hard work is needed in the near future. The last tile is most interesting, since it was Rho, and signifies a stop sign to wait a while before proceeding or to take a small break. I think that was prudent, and needed a break to reevaluate my life choices. I'm now in the process of applying for local law enforcement.

Just thought I'd share my divination results here.

But there's another topic I'd like to consider, and it's acedia. Acedia, spiritual apathy, seems to be something that I have been struggling with for some time now. It's not depression; I'm very happy with life, go to the gym, etc., but it's specifically spirituality-related. Doing morning and evening prayers and rituals become a chore, and are neglected, and a general spiritual malaise descends. It comes and goes, but I would love to know ways to combat it. I've read some of what Galina Krasskova has had to say about Acedia, and it's very good, but I wish more polytheists would talk about it (on Twitter, it seems that many suffer from it) and ways to fight the "noon-day demon"

Thanks again for all your work,
Daniel

replies.title: Re: Acedia, among other things
replies.content:
Well, I'm sorry to hear that fell through for you!

With regard to acedia, in my experience, when people find spirituality becoming a chore, it's because they're trying to push themselves faster than they're ready to go. Intensive spiritual practice is not for everyone; it may be better right now for you to spend your time engaging with the ordinary world rather than trying to pass beyond it.

replies.title: Re: Acedia, among other things
replies.content:
You know, about a year ago I was also trying to go into the military (national guard) but didn't make it due to an injury...twisted my ankle. I was really disappointed since I've always wanted to be in the security services, but I took it as a sign and have doubled down on my magical practice. I'm also in the martial arts and ended up with a trade job that may eventually allow me to enter the security industry from a totally unexpected direction. Magical practitioners seem to end up where they want to go, taking a road that they never expected because that's the road they need to take.

Regards,

Syfen

comments.title: Findhorn
comments.content: I would be really interested to know what occult or magical practices that Mr. Crombie and the other Findhorn founders used as they learned to communicate with the diva's or elementals.  The Findhorn book isn't to clear on that only that it was rigorous.

replies.title: Re: Findhorn
replies.content: There are actually quite a few Findhorn books. Which one did you have in mind?

comments.title: Communing with Coyotes
comments.content:
I'm a long-term pagan with a wide range of experiences. I recently moved to a 6.5 acre piece of land on the Kitsap Peninsula. We have deer, rabbits, birds, and of course coyotes, who are getting ever more widespread in the last couple of decades.

Okay, my dilemma: We have chickens and cats, and ONE coyote is breaking all the normal rules and hunting on our property in broad daylight, even with human activity a few dozen feet away! Never seen a coyote so bold! I really try to 'link' with any land I live on, and it's inhabitants. All are people to me, from trees to predators. So I saw the coyote in question waiting for me to let the cats out one morning and we stared at one another. I reached out with my mind for a chat. I tell this coyote (I named him "Dash" in my mind) to please help himself to all the rabbits and mice and such, but asked him, could he please stop going after our 'family' animals?

I wasn't sure what to expect from this interchange, and to my surprise I got an answer! He wanted one more chicken! And then, 2 days later, he got it. We're down 3 hens (yes, practical steps like better fencing now in the works) and the cats and chickens are on lock-down for now.

Do you have any further advice for striking a bargain with or in any way energetically dissuading Dash the Bold Coyote from continuing in this manner? Was my approach, asking him to leave off and otherwise have free reign on this land, the right one? I feel the spirit of things and try to contact them, and I felt the coyote answer me, but cultural influences make me distrust my intuition on this. Practical measures are being taken, but I honestly LIKE coyotes keeping the rabbit and raccoon populations in check. A healthy system needs predators. And this one is smart and very healthy and I rather like him, despite not being happy in regards to having to protect animals under my care.

Any ideas from the Druid perspective?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Communing with Coyotes
replies.content: It really does depend on the individual coyote. Some people, human or otherwise, can be bargained with; others can't. Since I can't sit down with this particular coyote for a chat, I can't tell you which of these categories he belongs to.

replies.title: Re: Communing with Coyotes
replies.content:
I guess I was wondering if there were any further techniques I could try, but perhaps not. I'm hoping the answer I 'heard' of it wanting one more chicken means that's it.

Better fencing is definitely happening, regardless!

replies.title: Re: Communing with Coyotes
replies.content: I can't speak to the bargains willingness of said coyote, but perhaps you can sweeten the bargain, if you're in a position to do so, by cultivating a portion of the land to "wildness" if it's been relatively flattened by other people - so, effectively make more habitat for mice, voles, rabbits, ground squirrels or other coyote treats. But perhaps it's relatively untrammeled already, in which case this isn't that relevant :)

replies.title: Re: Communing with Coyotes
replies.content: 4 of the 6.5 acres are wild, and we're completely fine with that. We just eke out our own gardens and suck on the 2 acres by the road. The entire area is relatively un "developed" and so wildlife thrives here.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi,

How can someone tell if they performed a ritual correctly or not? I did the LBRP for the first time time today, but I'm not sure if it worked. My heart rate went up, though. Neat.

Thank you again for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The only way to find out is to practice it daily, so that you develop the skills that will allow you to perceive its effects!

comments.title: Questions from a beginner
comments.content:
Mr Greer,

I have four questions.

1. What kinds of magic should be avoided in a home with a young child (under the age of three)?

2. Is it safe to practice these forms of magic elsewhere outside the home?

3. When I was around 14 years old, a friend who was an occultist taught me how to pull energy into my body using my left hand and transmit it from my right (or that's what he claimed he was doing). To this day I still feel a kind of pressure in my right palm much of the time. What is this?

4. When do you expect to be finished with the Heathen version of your Golden Dawn curriculum?

Thanks very much. I really enjoy your work.

replies.title: Re: Questions from a beginner
replies.content:
1) Sigh. I've literally answered this question every other week or so, and I'm not sure why everyone remembers "there's something you shouldn't do with children under the age of three" and nobody seems to remember what it is. I'm honestly starting to wish I'd simply said "No magic with children in the house" and left it at that -- though no doubt then I'd get people asking "So what was it we weren't supposed to do with children in the house?"

So I think I'm just going to tell you not to do magic at all until your children are more than three years old. if you want a more nuanced answer, by all means go looking through past Magic Mondays.

3) Good question. Since I don't know what technique he taught you and can't examine you personally, I have no way of knowing.

4) It's at least a year off. We have a lot of developmental work still to do.

replies.title: Re: Questions from a beginner
replies.content:
perhaps a short post on using www.google.com to search a site for a specific topic.

entering the following:

site:ecosophia.dreamwidth.org child + "magic monday"

into the search box for Google will return a nice listing of magic monday entries that include the word child.

Works well for searching ecosophia.net as well! ;)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

you said sigils only use the practitioner's energy. What else for which this is true do you know/cover in your books? May be relevant for Nordic Golden Dawn ("trúa á mátt sinn og megin").

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The style of magic I practice basically doesn't use the practitioner's energy all by itself. In Golden Dawn magic you're always putting yourself into harmony with divine energies when you do a working. One of the classic GD obligations, in fact, requires you to invoke divine powers consonant with the working whenever you do magical work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Does that mean taking up Golden Dawn magic intentionally restricts what else you can practice? (As you've done non-GD magic in the past, as I understand.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Every magical system limits you in some way, and the more narrow the limits the more power you have within them. The Golden Dawn system involves several quite specific limits -- you'll find them listed in the obligations of the Neophyte, Portal, and Adeptus Minor grades -- and that's one of the reasons it's so effective.

I actually did GD magic first, and then -- after some 20 years of systematic GD practice -- explored some other systems in order to enrich my understanding of magic. Now I've returned to a variant of the GD system, the one taught in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn, and am busy enriching the tradition with what I learned elsewhere.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thanks!

- S. T. Silva.

comments.title: Auras and natural magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Last week you mentioned that drug abuse can damage a person's aura. Could you speak more about that? I'm pretty sure I've experienced the effects of that in a romantic partner, but without realizing what was going on.

Also, since I'm working through the Druid Magic Handbook, would you advise holding off on any of the material in the Encyclopedia of Natural Magic?

Thanks,
Cliff

replies.title: Re: Auras and natural magic
replies.content:
1) The nature of the effects varies dramatically with the drug being used and the frequency of use. Can you describe the effects you experienced?

2) Yep. One thing at a time.

comments.title: Help! Neighbor trouble
comments.content:
I hope I make it under the wire for Monday, if I type fast enough.

A new neighbor across the street has been getting into the habit of yakking loudly on his cell phone for forty minutes or so while perambulating up and down the sidewalk in front of his house. This evening I found him doing it on my side of the street, in front of *my* house. I find this to be very annoying behavior. And it’s clear that a habitual pattern is establishing itself.

Long experience with this neighborhood tells me that politely asking him to desist is probably a bad idea. As I think you’re aware, the Bay Area is full of rich self-centered jerks, and the signs are that he’s probably one of them - an overpaid tech worker, I’m guessing. I’ve had some amazingly bad experiences with neighbors here - confrontations the ugliness of which surpasses belief.

That leaves me with maybe magic for shooing away this annoying individual from in front of my house..? I am all agog to learn of any suggestions you might propose.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Help! Neighbor trouble
replies.content: Have you considered a little hotfoot powder on the sidewalk? Alternatively, since the place you live is full of rich self-centered jerks, have you considered relocating?

replies.title: Re: Help! Neighbor trouble
replies.content:
I can’t afford to move. I’ll try the hotfoot powder. Thanks!

Kevin

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
You described somewhere (hopefully I remember it right) something to the effect that in early magical training (around 6 months in?) the ritual practice becomes tedious for a time, as the psychological effects wear off, and then there is a point where the student freaks out because they feel something…else.

I have been practicing according to the Druid Magic Handbook for 9 months, and the effects of the SOP have seemed pretty consistent the whole time. Yet I do feel a distinct ‘texture’ to each gate. I am curious if that is the something else, or if I have yet to reach the tedium/freakout stage. My guess is that I’ve just developed at a different rhythm.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Very likely, yes. Different students learn at different speeds, and in different ways.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Is ’The Path of Druidry’ by Penny Billington any good (as a supplement to your Druidry books)?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: As additional reading, quite possibly, but I don't recommend mixing practices from different systems, and what Penny teaches and what I teach are unquestionably different systems.
